# Arts & Culture

Literature, film, music, and cultural events from the year 2007.

<!-- BEGIN GENERATED: category-index — maintained by build-structure; do not edit by hand -->

| Topic | Summary |
|---|---|
| [Britney Spears Head-Shaving Incident](britney-spears-head-shaving-2007.md) | On February 16, Britney Spears shaved her head at a Los Angeles salon amid a personal crisis and custody battle, an image that became a defining moment in early-2000s celebrity-tabloid and mental-health discourse. |
| [Imus in the Morning Cancellation](imus-in-the-morning-cancellation.md) | Don Imus's April 4 racial remarks about the Rutgers women's basketball team drew civil-rights backlash and advertiser withdrawals, leading CBS Radio to cancel the long-running program on April 12. |
| [Live Earth 2007 Concerts](live-earth-2007.md) | On July 7 (7/7/07), more than 150 acts performed across twelve venues on all seven continents in a global benefit concert for climate-change awareness produced with Al Gore's Alliance for Climate Protection. |
| [Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows](harry-potter-deathly-hallows.md) | J. K. Rowling's seventh and final Harry Potter novel was published July 21, selling 8.3 million copies in 24 hours in the U.S. alone — a Guinness World Record — and concluding a decade-long publishing phenomenon. |
| [Writers Guild of America Strike](writers-guild-strike.md) | Approximately 12,000 WGA members struck from November 5, 2007 through February 12, 2008 over digital distribution residuals, halting U.S. television production for 100 days and reshaping residual-pay rules. |
| [No Country for Old Men](no-country-for-old-men.md) | The Coen Brothers' adaptation of Cormac McCarthy's novel went into wide release November 21 and won four Academy Awards — including Best Picture and Best Supporting Actor for Javier Bardem — at the 80th ceremony in February 2008. |
| [Second Life peaks as social and corporate phenomenon](second-life-2007.md) | In 2007 the virtual world Second Life reached its cultural and corporate peak, with roughly seven million registered accounts and heavy investment from IBM, Reuters, Sweden, and others, epitomizing the era's optimism about virtual worlds. |

<!-- END GENERATED: category-index -->
